Chunk # 0.
Mitochondria play diverse and important roles in
eukaryotic cells Besides their primary function of ATPproduction, mitochondria generate reactive oxygenspecies, serve as a major source of cellular NADH, par-ticipate in pyrimidine, lipid, and steroid hormonebiosynthesis, regulate cellular levels of metabolites,amino acids, and cofactors for various regulatoryenzymes, synthesize heme and Fe-S clusters, and partic-ipate in Ca
2+homeostasis, cell signaling, apoptosis, stress
response, epigenetic modulations, and one-carbonmetabolism [1-3] Therefore, it is not surprising thatmitochondria are involved in many human diseases, frominherited disorders caused by mutation in mitochondrialproteins to complex diseases like cancer, cardiomyo-
pathies, metabolic syndrome, and neurodegenerative dis-orders [4, 5] Despite some limitations, studies on isolated mito-
chondria remain instrumental for uncovering changes inthe mitochondrial proteome during pathological process-es, discovery of tumor neoantigens, and disease biomark-ers [6] Historically , technical papers on isolation of mito-
chondria have focused primarily on functionality andstructural integrity of the isolated organelles, but poorlyaddressed problems of maximizing yield and minimizingcontamination with non-mitochondrial components [7-9] Published methods allow purification of structurallyand functionally preserved mitochondria, but proteomicstudies often impose quite different demands For exam-ple, any organelle-targeted proteomic study would sig-nificantly suffer from contamination with other cellularcomponents Moreover, gel-based proteomics (e.g sero-logical proteome analysis) often involve multiple series ofelectrophoretic experiments and require large amountsof highly pure mitochondria to be isolated from a limitedISSN 0006-2979, Biochemistry (Moscow), 2018, Vol 83, No 1, pp 76-85 © Pleiades Publishing, Ltd., 2018.Originally published in Biochemistry (Moscow) On-Line Papers in Press, as Manuscript BM17-461, November 27, 2017 76Abbreviations : DC, differential centrifugation; DC+MACS,
magnetic separation of crude mitochondria fraction obtainedby DC; MACS, magnetic separation performed in full compli-ance with the Miltenyi Biotec protocol; Sonication+DC+MACS, modification of the DC+MACS protocol in whichhomogenization in hypoosmotic buffer was replaced by sonica-tion in sucrose buffer; WCL, whole cell lysate * To whom correspondence should be addressed.Isolation of Large Amounts of Highly Pure Mitochondria
for “Omics” Studies
M A Afanasyeva1*, A S Ustiugova1, S A Golyshev2, A T Kopylov3,
A V Bogolyubova1, D E Demin1,4, P V Belousov1, and A M Schwartz1,4
1Engelhardt Institute of Molecular Biology, Russian Academy of Sciences,
119991 Moscow, Russia; E-mail: ama@eimb.ru, m.a.afanasyeva@gmail.com
2Lomonosov Moscow State University, Belozersky Institute of Physico-Chemical Biology, 119234 Moscow, Russia
3Orekhovich Institute of Biomedical Chemistry, Russian Academy of Medical Sciences, 119121 Moscow, Russia
4Moscow Institute of Physics and Technology, 141701 Dolgoprudny, Moscow Region, Russia
Received October 13, 2017
Revision received October 27, 2017
Abstract —Ultracentrifugation on a density gradient remains the only reliable way to obtain highly pure mitochondria prepa-
rations However, it is not readily available for any laboratory and has a serious disadvantage of providing low mitochondriayield, which can be critical when working with limited starting material Here we describe a combined method for isolationof mitochondria for proteomic studies that includes cell disruption by sonication, differential centrifugation, and magneticseparation Our method provides remarkable enrichment of mitochondrial proteins as compared to differential centrifuga-tion, magnetic separation, or their combination, and it enables the strongest depletion of cytoplasmic components, asassessed by two-dimensional electrophoresis, mass spectrometry , and Western blot It also doubles the yield of mitochon-dria However, our method should not be used for functional studies as most of the isolated organelles demonstrate disturbedstructure in electron microphotographs DOI : 10.1134/S0006297918010108
Keywords : mitochondria isolation, proteomics, two-dimensional gel electrophoresisISOLATION OF MITOCHONDRIA 77
BIOCHEMISTRY  (Moscow)   V ol  83   No  1   2018source (slowly growing adherent cell line, primary cells,
patient’s tissue, etc.) while being indifferent to preserv-ing their functionality  To our knowledge, no suitablemethod satisfying these needs has been published sofar The three popular approaches to isolation of mito-
chondria from mammalian tissues and cells are: (i) differ-ential centrifugation (DC), (ii) ultracentrifugation on adensity gradient, and (iii) magnetic separation using anti-TOM22 antibodies (MACS) The first method is simple,fast, cheap, and gives the highest yield of mitochondria,but the resulting preparation is heavily contaminated withmicrosomal fraction and other cellular components.Ultracentrifugation gives much purer mitochondria, butthe yield is lower, the method is more laborious, and itrequires large and expensive equipment [10] MACS is afast and simple method of mitochondria isolation, butthere are conflicting reports on the purity of the resultingmitochondria fraction In the initial report by Hornig-Doet al [11], this method was shown to provide mitochon-dria almost as pure as can be achieved by ultracentrifuga-tion, with the advantage of higher yield and simplicity .However, an independent research group found that themitochondria fraction isolated using the Miltenyi BiotecMitochondria Isolation Kit based on the Hornig-Do pro-tocol showed very poor enrichment of the targetorganelles and the resulting preparation was highly con-taminated by nuclear and endoplasmic reticulum compo-nents [10] Using two-dimensional gel electrophoresis (2D-
PAGE) and mass spectrometry , we tested several modifi-cations of the magnetic separation protocol for mito-chondria isolation and describe herein an improvedmethod that yields large quantities of pure mitochondriasuitable for “omics” studies without the need for ultra-centrifugation MATERIALS AND METHODS

mitochondria.protocol:
1. "Ultracentrifugation on a density gradient remains the only reliable way to obtain highly pure mitochondria preparations."
2. "Here we describe a combined method for isolation of mitochondria for proteomic studies that includes cell disruption by sonication, differential centrifugation, and magnetic separation."
3. "Our method provides remarkable enrichment of mitochondrial proteins as compared to differential centrifugation, magnetic separation, or their combination, and it enables the strongest depletion of cytoplasmic components."
4. "It also doubles the yield of mitochondria."
5. "However, our method should not be used for functional studies as most of the isolated organelles demonstrate disturbed structure in electron microphotographs."

**Conclusion:** The article describes a combined method for isolating mitochondria for proteomic studies. The method includes cell disruption by sonication, differential centrifugation, and magnetic separation. It provides enrichment of mitochondrial proteins and depletion of cytoplasmic components, but it may not be suitable for functional studies due to disturbed organelle structure. The article does not provide a complete step-by-step protocol for the isolation process.

summary: Summary 4: Mitochondria, the powerhouses of eukaryotic cells, have diverse roles beyond ATP production. They generate reactive oxygen species, participate in biosynthesis, regulate metabolite levels, and synthesize heme and Fe-S clusters. Mitochondria are involved in calcium homeostasis, cell signaling, apoptosis, stress response, epigenetic modulations, and one-carbon metabolism. Mutations in mitochondrial proteins contribute to inherited disorders and complex diseases like cancer. Studies on isolated mitochondria have led to the discovery of tumor neoantigens and disease biomarkers. However, obtaining highly pure mitochondria for proteomic studies is challenging due to contamination with cellular components. Common methods for mitochondria isolation include ultracentrifugation on a density gradient and magnetic separation. Assessing purity and enrichment of mitochondrial proteins is done through two-dimensional electrophoresis, mass spectrometry, Western blot, and electron microphotographs.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. Cells were harvested using 0.05% trypsin/EDTA solution and washed with PBS.
2. The pellet was washed three times by resuspension in ice-cold sucrose buffer (0.25 M sucrose, 10 mM HEPES, pH 7.5) and centrifugation at 1000 g, 4°C, for 5 min.
3. Cells were harvested using 0.05% trypsin/EDTA solution, washed with PBS, and resuspended in ice-cold hypoosmotic solution (10 mM Tris-HCl, pH 6.7, 10 mM KCl, 15 mM MgCl2) at 10 ml per gram of cell pellet, and left to swell for 5-10 min.
4. The cells were then destroyed by 10 strokes in a glass-Teflon Potter-Elvehjem homogenizer put in ice.
5. Sucrose solution (2 M) was added to the final concentration of 0.25 M, and nuclei were pelleted by centrifugation at 1200 g, 4°C, for 5 min.
6. The supernatant was collected and centrifuged again under the same conditions to minimize contamination with the nuclear fraction.
7. The mitochondria were then pelleted from the supernatant by centrifugation at 10,000 g, 4°C, for 10 min.
8. Magnetic separation using anti-TOM22 antibodies was performed using the Human Mitochondria Isolation Kit.
9. Cells were harvested using 0.05% trypsin/EDTA solution, pelleted, washed with PBS, and resuspended in 1 ml of ice-cold lysis buffer provided with the kit.
10. From 80 to 85% cell destruction was achieved by passing the suspension through a 26.5G syringe 15 times on ice.
11. Further procedures were performed according to the manufacturer's protocol, including an optional step of passing the antibody-labeled mitochondria through a 40 μm mesh before applying to the column.
12. Differential centrifugation + magnetic separation were performed.
13. The mitochondria pellet obtained by differential centrifugation was resuspended in 1 ml of lysis buffer provided with the Mitochondria Isolation Kit.
14. The mitochondria were labeled with anti-TOM22 antibodies and isolated according to the Miltenyi Biotec protocol with minor modifications.
15. An additional wash of the column was performed with 20 ml of 0.5% BSA in PBS before collecting the mitochondria.
16. An additional flushing step with 2 ml of Separation buffer was added to increase the yield of mitochondria.
17. Sonication + differential centrifugation + magnetic separation were performed.
18. Cells were trypsinized, washed with PBS, resuspended in 4 ml of ice-cold sucrose buffer, and sonicated.
19. Pelleting of nuclear fraction and further steps were identical to the differential centrifugation + magnetic separation protocol.
20. After isolation by either procedure, the mitochondria were washed three times by adding the sucrose buffer to the pellet, extensive vortexing, and centrifugation at 13,000 g, 4°C, for 2 min.
21. The mitochondria and cell pellets were stored at -80°C.
22. Two-dimensional gel electrophoresis (2D-PAGE) was performed.
23. Mitochondria were lysed in isoelectrofocusing (IEF) buffer without bromophenol blue and sonicated.
24. The lysate was centrifuged, and the supernatant was transferred to a new tube.
25. Protein concentration was measured by the Bradford assay.

summary:
Summary (Iteration 4):
FTC-133, a follicular thyroid carcinoma cell line, was purchased from the Health Protection Agency Culture Collection. Whole cell lysate (WCL) preparation involved harvesting cells using trypsin/EDTA solution, washing with PBS, and resuspending the pellet in ice-cold sucrose buffer. Differential centrifugation (DC) was performed to destroy cells using a glass-Teflon homogenizer and isolate the mitochondria. Magnetic separation using anti-TOM22 antibodies (MACS) was also employed. Another method, sonication + differential centrifugation + magnetic separation (Sonication+DC+MACS), was used to isolate cells and perform two-dimensional gel electrophoresis (2D-PAGE) using IEF buffer.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. Equilibrate the strips for 20 min in equilibration buffer (6 M urea, 0.375 M Tris-HCl, pH 8.8, 20% glycerol, 2% SDS, 2% DTT).
2. Run the second dimension in 1-mm mini-gels (10 or 11% acrylamide/bis-acrylamide, 29:1) at 20 mA per gel.
3. Perform electrophoretic experiments in triplicates for each isolation method.
4. Stain the gels with freshly-prepared colloidal Coomassie blue (10% ammonium sulfate, 0.1% Coomassie blue G-250, 3% phosphoric acid, 20% ethanol) overnight on a shaker.
5. Wash the stained gels with deionized water for 6 h.
6. Scan the gels and store them in 1% acetic acid at 4°C until excision of protein spots.
7. Manually classify spots into five categories according to their intensities.
8. Excise spots of interest from 2D gels using pipet tips.
9. Wash the gel fragments twice with 40% methanol/10% glacial acetic acid for 15 min at 45°C with vigorous shaking.
10. Rinse the gel fragments three times for 3 min each with deionized water at 30°C with shaking at 500 rpm.
11. Equilibrate the gel fragments in 50 mM triethylammonium bicarbonate buffer (pH 8.0) and dry them under vacuum at 45°C for 60-90 min.
12. Rehydrate the dry gel fragments in trypsin inactivating solution (50 ng/μl in 30 mM acetic acid) at 4°C for 5 min.
13. Add 75 mM triethylammonium bicarbonate buffer (pH 8.0) to the gel fragments.
14. Digest the protein samples with trypsin (18,611 U/mg) for 4 h.
15. Stop the reaction by adding 1% acetic acid.
16. Extract peptides by three consecutive changes (15 min each) of 1% trifluoroacetic acid at increasing temperatures (45-65°C).
17. Pool the collected fractions together and dry them under vacuum at 30°C.
18. Redissolve the pellet in 10-15 μl of 0.5% formic acid and transfer it to glass inserts for LC-MS analysis.
19. Perform liquid chromatographic (LC) separation using an UltiMate 3000 RSLCnano System.
20. Separate the peptides on an Acclaim RSLC PepMapC18 analytical column coupled with an enrichment C18 PepMap μ-precolumn.
21. Load the peptides onto the μ-precolumn in mobile phase C (2.5% acetonitrile, 0.08% formic acid, 0.03% acetic acid, pH 2.66) for 4 min at 15 μl/min flow rate.
22. Separate the peptides in an eluting gradient of mobile phase A (0.08% formic acid, 0.03% acetic acid, pH 2.70) and mobile phase B (0.08% formic acid, 0.03% acetic acid in acetonitrile).
23. Start with 2% mobile phase B for 3.5 min and increase the B concentration linearly up to 35% over 37.5 min.
24. Hold the column in isocratic mode for 4 min with 35% mobile phase B.
25. Wash the column with 98% mobile phase B for 7 min at 0.45

summary: The strips were equilibrated in an inequilibrium buffer for 20 minutes. The second dimension was run in mini-gels. Electrophoretic experiments were performed in triplicates. Gels were stained with Coomassie blue overnight and washed. Spots were manually classified into five categories based on their intensities. In-gel tryptic digestion and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) analysis were performed. Spots of interest were excised from 2D gels. The fragments were washed and equilibrated. The protein samples were digested with trypsin. Peptides were extracted and collected fractions were pooled. Liquid chromatographic separation was performed. Mass spectrometric analysis was conducted. Proteins were identified using the MASCOT search engine. Transmission electron microscopy was performed on a mitochondria pellet.

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation were mentioned in the article, but the protocol is not complete. Here are the extracted steps:

1. "Among many published methods of mitochondria isolation, most are based on DC, and many commercial kits also employ this principal."
2. "The only alternative that does not require specialized and expensive equipment (as opposed to ultracentrifugation and free-flow electrophoresis) is magnetic separation using anti-TOM22 antibodies."
3. "Therefore, we first analyzed the proteomic profiles of mitochondria isolated by these two fundamentally different techniques requiring only basic equipment: DC and the original Miltenyi Biotec protocol of MACS and compared them to electrophoretic pattern of WCL."
4. "As Fig 2 (upper row) and table illustrate, both DC and the original MACS protocols did not provide satisfactory elimination of non-mitochondrial proteins ENO1, YWHAZ, YWHAE, and TPM4 in DC preparation, HSP90AB1 in MACS preparation, and ACTB in both DC and MACS preparations did not show any depletion compared to WCL."
5. "Moreover, ANXA2 was enriched in the DC preparation."
6. "In general, the MACS protocol performed slightly better, but contamination with non-mitochondrial proteins remained unacceptably high."
7. "As the principle of magnetic separation was expected to enable isolation of very pure organelles, we supposed that nuclei and cell debris could be nonspecifically stuck in columns, and low-speed pre-centrifugation was expected to solve this problem."
8. "To this end, we added an additional step to the original Miltenyi Biotec protocol – centrifugation of cell lysate at 1000 g for 5 min prior to incubation with anti-TOM22 antibodies."
9. "This modification of the protocol resulted in 2-fold decrease in mitochondria yield and would have led to unjustified waste of columns."
10. "To overcome this problem, we used crude mitochondria fraction obtained by DC as a starting material for magnetic separation, which was also expected to improve purity."
11. "Contrary to our expectations, combined DC+MACS protocol did not substantially improve the purity of mitochondria."
12. "Some proteins (HSP90AB1, VIME) demonstrated enrichment as compared to DC, MACS, or even WCL."

Conclusion: The article describes a comparison between differential centrifugation (DC) and magnetic separation using anti-TOM22 antibodies (MACS) for mitochondrial isolation. The steps mentioned include analyzing proteomic profiles, identifying non-mitochondrial protein contamination, adding a low-speed pre-centrifugation step, and using crude mitochondria fraction as starting material for magnetic separation. However, the article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation, and there are uncertainties regarding the effectiveness of the combined DC+MACS protocol.

summary:
Summary 4: The sample was placed in pure Epon-812 resin (SPI Inc.) and cured for 72 hours at 70°C. Ultrathin 90-nm sections were prepared using a Reicher-Jung Ultracut Eultramicrotome (Reichert-Jung) equipped with a diamond knife. The sections were mounted on formvar-coated copper slot grids (Ted Pella) and contrasted with 2% aqueous uranyl acetate and lead citrate. They were then examined and photographed using a JEM-1400 electron microscope (JEOL Inc.) equipped with a Quemesa CCD (OSIS) and run at 100 kV.

For the Western blot, whole cell or mitochondria lysates were mixed with 5 ×Laemmli buffer, boiled for 5 min, and loaded onto a 10% polyacrylamide gel. Antibodies against RNA polymerase II (ab5095), histone H3 (No 9715), β-actin (Cell Signaling), GRP 78 (sc-13968), and SWI/SNF complex subunit SMARCC1 (P155) were used, along with HRP-conjugated goat anti-rabbit IgG (No 31460) as the secondary antibody. Mitochondrial DNA content was quantified using real-time PCR.

Among different methods of mitochondria isolation, magnetic separation using anti-TOM22 antibodies was compared to differential centrifugation (DC) and the original MACS protocol. Both DC and MACS methods did not provide satisfactory elimination of non-mitochondrial proteins (ENO1, YWHAZ, YWHAE, TPM4, HSP90AB1, ACTB). A modification of the MACS protocol with low-speed pre-centrifugation was attempted, but it did not substantially improve the purity of mitochondria.

The study highlights the use of Epon-812 resin (SPI Inc.), Reicher-Jung Ultracut Eultramicrotome (Reichert-Jung), formvar-coated copper slot grids (Ted Pella), JEM-1400 electron microscope (JEOL Inc.) equipped with a Quemesa CCD (OSIS), and 5 ×Laemmli buffer for Western blot analysis. Antibodies against RNA polymerase II (ab5095), histone H3 (No 9715), β-actin (Cell Signaling), GRP 78 (sc-13968), and SWI/SNF complex subunit SMARCC1 (P155) were used, along with HRP-conjugated goat anti-rabbit IgG (No 31460) as the secondary antibody. Real-time PCR was used to quantify mitochondrial DNA content.

The study also discusses the challenges in isolating mitochondria and quantifying mitochondrial DNA content using Western blot and real-time PCR. The MACS protocol, which involves magnetic separation using anti-TOM22 antibodies, was compared to differential centrifugation (DC) and found to have similar issues with non-mitochondrial protein contamination. A modification of the MACS protocol with low-speed pre-centrifugation did not substantially improve the purity of mitochondria.

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for mitochondrial isolation from the article are as follows:

1. "Sonication in sucrose buffer was shown to be the best cell disruption method for proteomic studies of mitochondria among many tested options, providing high yield and purity."
2. "Chaiyarit and Thongboonkerd subjected cultured cells to sonication in sucrose buffer using a Bandelin Sonopuls HD 200 homogenizer equipped with MS 72/D for 3, 5, and 10 s (50% cycle), and chose 10-s regimen as the best option."
3. "We compared this cell disruption method with classical homogenization in hypoosmotic buffer using a Potter–Elvehjem homogenizer, as well as with two extended sonication regimens (15 and 20 s)."
4. "Cell disruption level and mitochondria yield increased with increasing sonication time from 10 to 15 s, and it remained almost unchanged with further increase, reaching up to 90 mg of mitochondria per gram of wet cell mass at 20 s (mean ± SD = 55.7 ± 13.4)."
5. "This was 7.7 times more than using hypoosmotic buffer and Potter–Elvehjem homogenizer (mean ± SD = 11.7 ± 2.6)."
6. "Replacing the Potter–Elvehjem homogenization with sonication remarkably altered protein spot intensities and even the presence in 2D gels, whereas duration of sonication did not matter."
7. "We analyzed 22 protein spots whose intensity or presence substantially differed between Sonication+DC+MACS and other protocols using LC-MS."
8. "Of those, 21 spots could be reliably identified as containing either only mitochondrial or only non-mitochondrial proteins."
9. "Eight spots referred to one or several mitochondrial proteins, and their intensities were greatly enhanced after sonication."
10. "Thirteen spots corresponded to 10 non-mitochondrial proteins."
11. "Of those, HSP90AB1, ENO1, VIME, YWHAZ, YWHAE, TPM4, CTSB, and ANXA2 became almost undetectable after sonication."
12. "ACTB (β-actin), which was very abundant in all other preparations, showed great reduction in quantity."
13. "LMNA demonstrated enrichment after sonication."
14. "To clarify if other nuclear proteins were also enriched in the Sonication+DC+MACS preparation, we used Western blot to measure abundance of DNA-directed RNA polymerase II, SWI/SNF complex subunit SMARCC1, and histone H3 in mitochondria samples isolated by the tested protocols."
15. "We found that unlike LMNA gene products, polymerase II and chromatin remodeling complex subunit SMARCC1 were largely depleted in the Sonication+DC+MACS as well as in the DC and DC+MACS preparations."
16. "Histone H3 was present in all mitochondria samples, being most abundant in the Sonication+DC+MACS sample."
17. "Thus, the Sonication+DC+MACS preparation appeared to be substantially contaminated only with extremely abundant nuclear proteins."
18. "One possible explanation for this finding is that sonication causes DNA fragmentation and stronger destruction of nuclei compared to Potter–Elvehjem or syringe homogenization, thus releasing more nuclear proteins that further nonspecifically contaminate the mitochondria fraction."

Conclusion: The article provides a detailed description of the sonication method for mitochondrial isolation, including the specific equipment used and the duration of sonication. It compares this method with classical homogenization and extended sonication regimens. The article also mentions the effects of sonication on protein spot intensities and the presence of nuclear proteins in the isolated mitochondria fraction

summary: Summary 5: Chaiyarit and Thongboonkerd conducted proteomic studies of mitochondria using cultured cells. They compared sonication in sucrose buffer using the Bandelin Sonopuls HD 200 homogenizer with homogenization in hypoosmotic buffer using a Potter-Elvehjem homogenizer. Sonication for 10 seconds provided the highest yield and purity of mitochondria, resulting in up to 90 mg of mitochondria per gram of wet cell mass. The Sonication+DC+MACS preparation enhanced the intensity of mitochondrial proteins, including HSP90AB1, ENO1, VIME, YWHAZ, YWHAE, TPM4, CTSB, ANXA2, and LMNA. Sonication caused DNA fragmentation and released nuclear proteins, contaminating the mitochondria fraction. Differential centrifugation and magnetic separation techniques were used to purify mitochondria samples. The protocols for differential centrifugation and magnetic separation were modified for optimal results. Further details can be found in the publication "AFANASYEVA et al BIOCHEMISTRY (Moscow) Vol 83 No 1 2018".

mitochondria.protocol:
Based on the provided text, it is difficult to extract the complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation. However, we can extract some steps and note the incompleteness and uncertainties. 

1. The best-performing protocol is 20 s Sonication+DC+MACS.
2. The protocol involves cell disruption by sonication.
3. Differential centrifugation is performed.
4. Magnetic separation is performed using the MACS protocol.

Notes:
- The specific details of the sonication, differential centrifugation, and magnetic separation steps are not provided.
- The article mentions that the mitochondria obtained by the Sonication+DC+MACS protocol were generally nonfunctional and should not be used for studying their activity.
- The article suggests that the Sonication+DC+MACS procedure provides high enrichment of mitochondrial proteins and depletion of cytoplasmic components, but it does not provide details on the effectiveness of this procedure.

Conclusion:
The provided text does not provide a complete and detailed protocol for mitochondrial isolation. While it mentions the use of sonication, differential centrifugation, and magnetic separation, the specific steps and conditions are not described. Additionally, the article suggests that the Sonication+DC+MACS protocol yields nonfunctional mitochondria. Further information is needed to obtain a comprehensive and reliable protocol for mitochondrial isolation.

summary: Summary 4: The MACS preparation, which contains nuclear proteins, clogs the column and is a concern for contamination. Mitochondria with varying integrity are found in the WCL. Mitochondria with well-preserved cristae have intact outer and inner membranes. Voltage-dependent anion-selective channel proteins 1 and 2, dihydrolipoyllysine-residue succinyl transferase, ornithine aminotransferase, and calcium-binding mitochondrial carrier protein SCaMC-12 are present in mitochondria. Contamination of mitochondria preparations with non-mitochondrial proteins is evaluated. Pol II, P155, and H3 are present in mitochondria. Swollen and nonfunctional mitochondria are observed. Microsomal contamination and single-membrane vesicles are detected. Mitochondrial DNA content is measured using real-time PCR. Mitochondria-derived fragments and cytoplasmic components are present. Purity of mitochondria is assessed using mass spectrometric measurements. The MACS preparation contains nuclear proteins and clogs the column. Endoplasmic reticulum proteins are not found in the mitochondria samples. The amount of GRP 78 in mitochondria samples is evaluated. DC+MACS and Sonication+DC+MACS decrease the amount of GRP 78. The Sonication+DC+MACS protocol provides the best-performing mitochondria preparation. Mitochondria are classified into three categories based on integrity. Organelles with well-preserved cristae comprise 37% of the total. Organelles with distorted cristae comprise 39% of the total. Double-membrane vesicles likely represent mitochondrial debris. Mitochondria obtained by the Sonication+DC+MACS protocol are generally nonfunctional. Microsomal contamination is excluded. Single-membrane vesicles are present in low numbers. Fragments of the nuclear envelope are not readily recognizable. Enrichment of mitochondria by the Sonication+DC+MACS protocol is quantified. Mitochondrial DNA content is measured using real-time PCR, showing a 3-fold enrichment. The Sonication+DC+MACS procedure provides high enrichment of mitochondrial proteins and doubles the yield of mitochondria. The Sonication+DC+MACS fraction is contaminated with histone H3 and lamins. DC results in low contamination with low-abundance nuclear proteins and much lower contamination with histone H3 and lamins compared to Sonication+DC+MACS. The DC+MACS protocol does not have any advantages compared to DC and lowers mitochondria yield 2-fold. Most purified organelles lose their integrity. Quality control and optimization of protocols are performed to assess mitochondrial integrity, cytoplasmic components, purity, microsomal contamination, nonfunctional mitochondria, mitochondrial DNA content, and contamination. The Russian Science Foundation and the Moscow State University development program provide scientific research funding. Figures 3 and 4, supplementary Table S1, and Figure S2 in the Supplement provide additional information.

mitochondria.protocol:
**Isolation of Mitochondria Protocol:**

1. Varying degrees of mitochondria preservation can be observed.
2. Well-preserved mitochondria with electron-dense matrix.
3. Well-preserved mitochondria with electron-transparent matrix.
4. Mitochondria with distorted cristae.
5. Double- and single-membrane vesicles.

**Notes:**
- The article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation.
- The steps mentioned describe the varying degrees of mitochondria preservation, but do not provide specific isolation methods or techniques.
- The article references other sources that may provide more detailed protocols for mitochondrial isolation.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a description of the different degrees of mitochondria preservation but does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation. Researchers looking for a detailed protocol should refer to the references mentioned in the article.

summary: Revised Summary: Mitochondria, the powerhouses of the cell, exhibit varying degrees of preservation, with well-preserved mitochondria having electron-dense or electron-transparent matrices. Distorted cristae, double- and single-membrane vesicles, as well as magnetic beads and an unidentified contaminant (presumably precipitated BSA) are observed during mitochondria isolation. The isolation process involves the use of a separation buffer. Various researchers have contributed to the understanding of mitochondrial structure and function, including Nunnari, Suomalainen, Zong, Rabinowitz, White, D'Aquila, Bellizzi, Passarino, Palmfeldt, Bross, Picard, Wallace, Burelle, Eremina, Pashintseva, Kovalev, Kovaleva, Shishkin, Witter, Watson, Cottone, Weinbach, Kappler, Li J., Haring, Weigert, Lehmann, Xu, Hoene, Hornig-Do, Gunther, Bust, Lehnartz, Bosio, Wiesner, Rabilloud, Afanasyeva, Britanova, Korneev, Mitkin, Kuchmiy, Kuprash, Brechalov, Georgieva, Soshnikova, Wieckowski, Giorgi, Lebiedzinska, Duszynski, Pinton, Chaiyarit, Thongboonkerd, Geiger. Mitochondria play a crucial role in biochemistry, proteomics, cancer research, and medicine. The preservation and analysis of mitochondria, including lipid profiling and proteomic analysis, are important areas of study. The use of superparamagnetic microbeads and two-dimensional electrophoresis aid in the isolation and analysis of mitochondria. Clusterin, PBAF complexes, mitochondria-associated membranes, and cell disruption methods are also relevant in the study of mitochondria. Comparative proteomic analysis of common cell lines reveals varying expression of most proteins. The rise of mitochondria in medicine and the proteomics of mammalian mitochondria in health and malignancy are important areas of research. A procedure for isolating stable mitochondria from rat liver and kidney has been developed, and purity is crucial for high-resolution lipid profiling of mitochondria.
